title: Severe and Moderate Asthma Exacerbations in Asthmatic Children and Exposure to Ambient Air Pollutants.
ehr_evidence: Exacerbations of asthma... were deﬁned as one hospitalization or emergency room visit with a diagnosis of asthma The study used the Quebec Integrated Chronic Disease Surveillance System (QICDSS), which links the health insurance registry, medical services (physician claims), hospital admissions, and deaths. new cases of asthma were identified (using codes of the International Classification of Diseases) either by one hospital discharge with a diagnosis of asthma... or two physician claims for as
summary: Assessment of the association between birth and annual exposure to outdoor air pollutants and severe asthma exacerbations in a population-based cohort of asthmatic children in Quebec, Canada.
key_findings: Time-dependent exposure to NO2, O3, and PM2.5 was significantly associated with asthma exacerbations.
No significant association was found between exposure at birth to NO2 or O3 and asthma exacerbations.
The study assessed the relation between long-term time dependent or birth exposure to O3, NO2 and PM2.5 and severe exacerbation of asthma in asthmatic children.
Not explicitly stated in the provided text snippet.
22.03% of asthmatic children had at least one exacerbation.
Average follow-up time of 6.25 years for the Quebec cohort.
Moderate positive correlation between NO2 and PM2.5 (approx. 0.55).
Moderate inverse correlations between O3 and NO2/PM2.5.
NO2 at birth residential postal code was associated with asthma exacerbations, but the association was removed after controlling for social and material deprivation.
PM2.5 at birth residential postal code showed a persistent association with asthma exacerbations even after adjusting for deprivation.
O3 showed no significant association with asthma exacerbations.
Sex was a significant effect modifier for NO2 exposure, with girls showing a higher risk of exacerbation.
Positive and significant associations between severe asthma exacerbation and time-dependent PM2.5, NO2, and O3 levels.
No association found with exposure to NO2 and O3 at the birth residential postal code.
PM2.5 was the only air pollutant assessed for which exposure at birth was linked to an increase in risk of exacerbations during follow-up.
Exposure at birth to NO2 and O3 not linked to asthma exacerbations.
Prolonged exposure to NO2, PM2.5, and O3 is mainly related to exacerbation of asthma.
Associations between traffic exposure and asthma exacerbations were of a similar magnitude when defined by health care service use compared to self-reported symptoms.
Consistent associations between time-dependent exposure to PM2.5, O3, or NO2 and asthma exacerbation in asthmatic children.
Residential exposure to air pollution at birth was not associated with increased asthma exacerbation for O3 and NO2.
Not explicitly stated in the provided reference list.
